title: Unable to write to oom_score_adj from a privileged container
body:
### Issue Description

A privileged container running in a rootful podman machine (created with --rootful) cannot set oom_score_adj. It gets permission denied.

* The machine is rootful
* The container is privileged
* There are no SELinux errors reported in the machine
  - But I put SELinux in the machine in Permissive mode anyway, just in case
* I *can* write to oom_score_adj in the machine 

I am attempting to use this container/machine to administratively isolate a workload that I trust. Security is not an issue. Changing the behaviour of the workload is not in scope. i.e. it writes to oom_score_adj, so I need to find a way to let it.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1.
```
> podman machine init --rootful
Looking up Podman Machine image at quay.io/podman/machine-os:5.2 to create VM
Extracting compressed file: podman-machine-default-amd64.qcow2: done
Image resized.
Machine init complete
To start your machine run:

        podman machine start
```
2.
```
> PATH=$PATH:/usr/libexec podman machine start podman-machine-default
Starting machine "podman-machine-default"
Mounting volume... /home/mbooth:/home/mbooth
API forwarding listening on: /run/user/1000/podman/podman-machine-default-api.sock
You can connect Docker API clients by setting DOCKER_HOST using the
following command in your terminal session:

        export DOCKER_HOST='unix:///run/user/1000/podman/podman-machine-default-api.sock'

Machine "podman-machine-default" started successfully
```
3.
```
> podman -c podman-machine-default run -ti --rm --privileged quay.io/centos/centos:stream9 /bin/bash
[root@db295224546d /]# echo 0 > /proc/self/oom_score_adj
bash: echo: write error: Permission denied
```


### Describe the results you received

Permission denied

### Describe the results you expected

Not permission denied

### podman info output

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Privileged

### Upstream Latest Release

No

### Additional environment details

I am running from a non-root account under WSL2. I assume, however, that use of podman machine makes any quirks of WSL2 irrelevant.

### Additional information

_No response_

comments.body:
> podman -c podman-machine-default

You are connecting to the rootless socket. You have to use the `podman-machine-default-root` to connect the rootful socket. The init --rootful setting only changes the default connection. If you specify the connection by name you must use the right one.

A rootless container will not be able to increase limits so that is expected but if you actual run this as root it should work fine.

> > podman -c podman-machine-default
> 
> You are connecting to the rootless socket. You have to use the `podman-machine-default-root` to connect the rootful socket. The init --rootful setting only changes the default connection. If you specify the connection by name you must use the right one.
> 
> A rootless container will not be able to increase limits so that is expected but if you actual run this as root it should work fine.

This is the answer. Many thanks.

Incidentally, in my environment the default connection remains unchanged when creating a machine: it runs locally without using a machine. This is the only reason I was specifying a connection. If I don't specify a connection then it doesn't use the machine at all. Should this be considered a bug?

If your host runs on linux the default is to use the local host not a remote connection. You need to use `podman-remote` or `podman --remote` then it should use the default connection as listed in `podman system connection list`
